characters.physical_details: Average height, average build going slightly soft. Hair thinning at the crown but not bald — the kind of hair loss that doesn't qualify as a feature. Wears khakis and button-downs to work, the same three pairs rotated. Owns one suit, the one he wore to the wedding, which he wakes up in. Glasses — reading glasses that he needs but often forgets, not fashion glasses. No distinguishing marks. He looks like an insurance adjuster because he is one.

key_relationships.kelsey_bridge_coyle: daughter — loves her in a way he cannot articulate, which is the central wound
key_relationships.megan_coyle: ex-wife — married 18 years, divorced 12 years ago, complicated mixture of guilt and acceptance
key_relationships.brendan_coyle: ex-wife's husband — impossible to hate, which is what Doug hates
key_relationships.roy_utley: only real friend and coworker — the relationship that requires the least and gives the most
key_relationships.gloria_bridge: mother — the source of his bluntness, though his is passive where hers is active
key_relationships.layla_banks: manager — professional relationship tinged with mutual bewilderment
key_relationships.simone_barr: comedy writer — professional relationship that becomes the novel's most important intellectual and emotional connection

characters.occupation_status: Insurance adjuster at Mutual Fidelity, Columbus, Ohio. Specialty: residential water damage since 2014. Mid-level, competent, not ambitious. Has been there approximately 15 years.
characters.literacy_languages: English only. Reads contracts carefully and thoroughly. Does not read for pleasure often — reads claim documentation, policy language, and occasionally a novel Roy recommends.

state_transitions.change: Discovers his drunken toast has gone viral; containment breached
Signs management contract with Layla; commits to public path
Bombs at first paid gig; confronts that the toast was an event, not a skill
Learns about the spreadsheet from Kelsey; confronts his specific mode of failure as a father
Abandons script at Netflix taping; performs unscripted for sixty-three minutes
Returns to Mutual Fidelity; life slightly shifted, not transformed

characters.availability: Present in every chapter. Chapters are told from his perspective.

characters.physical_details: Resembles Megan more than Doug — dark hair, direct gaze. Carries herself with the composure of someone who decided early to be more organized than her parents' marriage. Wears scrubs at work. Dresses casually otherwise — jeans, simple tops, no effort to perform.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: father — loves him but is exasperated by his inability to say things directly except when drunk
key_relationships.megan_coyle: mother — closer to Megan logistically but emotionally more like Doug, which neither of them acknowledges
key_relationships.tyler_bridge_coyle: husband — recently married, uncertain whether she married stability or boredom
key_relationships.brendan_coyle: stepfather — cordial, appreciates his kindness, does not consider him a parent

characters.occupation_status: Dental hygienist. Works at a practice in Cleveland. Competent, professional, not particularly passionate about the work.

state_transitions.change: Confronts Doug about the toast's impact on her life; asks about the table line
Reveals the spreadsheet she found at sixteen; the deepest honesty between them
Watches Doug perform honest, unscripted material at the taping

characters.availability: Present in chapters 1 (text only), 5, 13, 17, 18 (text only). Lives in Cleveland, drives down to Columbus for visits.

characters.physical_details: Well-maintained, deliberate appearance. Dark hair, well-cut. Dresses with intention — not expensively but thoughtfully. She looks like someone who made decisions about her life and is living inside those decisions comfortably.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: ex-husband — fair to him in a way that feels, to Doug, like a specific cruelty
key_relationships.brendan_coyle: husband — genuinely loves him, chose him, does not doubt the choice
key_relationships.kelsey_bridge_coyle: daughter — close, but the closeness is logistical as much as emotional

characters.occupation_status: Works in nonprofit administration. Specific role not critical to plot.
characters.literacy_languages: English. College-educated. Reads therapy and self-help with genuine engagement, not performatively.

state_transitions.change: Responds to Doug's apology with organized calm; mentions Brendan's reaction
Mediates the dinner; her composure tested by the Doug-Brendan dynamic
Present at the taping as witness

characters.availability: Present in chapters 5 (phone), 8, 17, 18 (referenced). Lives in Columbus with Brendan.

characters.physical_details: Tall, fit in the way of a man who exercises regularly without obsession. Graying temples. Looks like a doctor — reassuring, composed, hands that suggest competence. Well-dressed in a casual, quality way.

key_relationships.megan_coyle: wife — loves her, aware of the complexity of replacing Doug
key_relationships.doug_bridge: wife's ex-husband — genuinely respects Doug in a way that is both real and devastating
key_relationships.kelsey_bridge_coyle: stepdaughter — cordial, appropriate, does not overstep

characters.occupation_status: Orthopedic surgeon. Successful practice. The income difference between him and Doug is significant and visible.

state_transitions.change: Reveals his own relationship to inadequacy — 'I couldn't have done what you did'

characters.availability: Present in chapters 5 (mentioned), 8, 17. Lives in Columbus with Megan. Their house is in a nicer neighborhood than Doug's apartment.

characters.physical_details: Small, kinetic, dressed in a way that suggests both hustle and taste — leather jacket, good boots, phone always in hand. Moves fast. Talks with her hands.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: client — her potential ticket to bigger representation
key_relationships.charlie_falk: Netflix contact — professional relationship, she's working the connection

characters.occupation_status: Talent manager. Runs a small management company. Current roster: mid-tier podcast hosts, a former Bachelorette contestant. Doug is her highest-profile client by a large margin.

state_transitions.change: Signs Doug; begins managing his public trajectory
Professional composure cracks as the taping approach reveals the material doesn't work

characters.availability: Present in chapters 4, 6 (arranges LA trip), 9 (arranges corporate gig), 12 (referenced), 14 (books tour), 16, 17. Based in New York, travels to Columbus as needed.

characters.physical_details: Unremarkable by design. Medium everything. Wears the office-appropriate version of whatever requires the least thought. His desk is clean. His sandwich order is consistent.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: only real friend and coworker — the relationship that functions because neither man asks too much of it

characters.occupation_status: Insurance adjuster at Mutual Fidelity. Same level as Doug. Has been there longer. Is not interested in advancement.
characters.literacy_languages: English. Reads one book a month from the library. No preferences.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 2, 3, 9 (phone), 10 (mentioned), 12, 16 (phone), 17 (absent, consistent), 18. Always at the office or reachable by phone. Does not travel.

characters.physical_details: California-casual. Looks younger than 44 in the way that people in entertainment do. Sneakers that cost more than Doug's suit. Enthusiastic gestures. Always has a notebook or phone for notes.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: development subject — the project he is tasked with turning into content
key_relationships.layla_banks: professional contact — they share the project of packaging Doug

characters.occupation_status: Development executive at Netflix. Mid-level but ambitious. Has developed several projects, few have made it to air.

state_transitions.change: Still enthusiastic even as the project collapses — his faith in the process is unshakable

characters.availability: Present in chapters 6, 16, 17. Based in LA. Comes to Columbus for the taping.

characters.physical_details: Comfortable in her appearance without performing comfort. Hair usually up. Dresses practically — she's here to work, not to be looked at. Drinks bourbon. Has the posture of someone who spends a lot of time watching and listening.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: client and emerging friend — the most important relationship in the novel after Kelsey

characters.occupation_status: Comedy writer. Has written for three late-night shows (most recently departed about two years ago). Freelance. Hired through Layla's network to develop Doug's material.
characters.literacy_languages: English. Deep knowledge of comedy history, structure, and craft.

state_transitions.change: Identifies Doug's comic voice — the precision gap between bureaucratic framing and human mess
Reveals her own backstory — a performer who moved to writing because performing was miserable

characters.availability: Present in chapters 7, 11, 12 (session observed by Alex), 14 (phone calls from each tour city), 15, 16, 17. Comes to Columbus from New York, stays for the duration of the development process.

characters.physical_details: Small, sharp-featured, white hair kept short. Moves deliberately — not frail but aware of her body in the way of someone her age. Eyes that miss nothing. Wears what she wears and does not consider alternatives.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: son — the only person she bothers being honest with, which is her form of love
key_relationships.harold_bridge: late husband (Doug's father) — died approximately 15 years ago, still referenced as a standard of comparison

characters.occupation_status: Retired. Lives at Maple Glen Senior Living in Dayton.
characters.literacy_languages: English. Reads the newspaper. Watches television selectively.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 10, 17, 18 (phone). Lives in Dayton, about 70 miles from Columbus.

characters.physical_details: Pleasant-looking. Clean-cut. The kind of face you recognize without remembering. Wears what software engineers wear.

key_relationships.doug_bridge: father-in-law — polite, uncertain how to engage

characters.occupation_status: Software engineer. Works for a company Doug cannot remember the name of.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 5 (mentioned), 13 (mentioned via Kelsey), 17 (at taping with Kelsey), 18 (mentioned in text). Lives in Cleveland with Kelsey.

characters.physical_details: Young, earnest-looking, carries a notebook and recorder. Dressed in a way that signals journalism without announcing it.
characters.occupation_status: Journalist at a major digital publication. Writing a profile of Doug.

timeline.story_start: Late winter/early spring, contemporary. The wedding took place the previous Saturday. The novel opens the morning after.
timeline.estimated_span: Approximately 5-6 months, from late winter through summer.

seasonal_track.season: Late winter — cold, gray Columbus. Doug wears a coat. Heating is on.
Early spring — LA is warm when Doug visits (chapter 6), Columbus is still cool. Trees budding.
Spring — warming, longer days. The Indianapolis trip and Cleveland/Pittsburgh/Detroit tour happen in spring weather.
Late spring/early summer — the taping is in early summer. Warm evenings. Doug stops wearing a coat.

key_events.event: Kelsey and Tyler's wedding; Doug's toast
key_events.chapter: Pre-novel, experienced via video in chapter 1

key_events.event: Special airs; Doug returns to normal life

geography.primary_setting: Columbus, Ohio — a mid-sized midwestern city. Not rural, not glamorous. College town energy (Ohio State) but the novel's characters are not connected to the university. Specific neighborhoods matter: Clintonville (Doug's apartment), Worthington (diner with Kelsey), the Short North (bar with Simone).

key_locations.name: Doug's apartment
key_locations.details: Two-bedroom in Clintonville. Second bedroom is an 'office' with no computer — just unpacked boxes from the move 12 years ago. Ikea bookshelf. One art print Megan left behind. Glass shower. Kitchen where he eats cereal standing at the counter.

key_locations.name: Mutual Fidelity office
key_locations.details: Beige open-plan space. Thirty adjusters. Doug's desk and Roy's desk are adjacent. Break room. The building is unremarkable — the kind of office building you drive past without noticing.

key_locations.name: The sandwich place
key_locations.details: Where Doug and Roy eat lunch. Nine years of the same routine. Not named or fancy — a local shop with a counter and maybe six tables.

key_locations.name: Megan and Brendan's house
key_locations.details: Nicer neighborhood than Clintonville. The house reflects Brendan's income — not ostentatious but comfortable. Good kitchen. Dining room where the dinner takes place. Doug has been inside four times in twelve years.

key_locations.name: Maple Glen Senior Living
key_locations.details: Gloria's retirement community in Dayton. About 70 miles from Columbus, roughly 1 hour drive. Has a communal dining room where Gloria's friends gather. Exactly as depressing as the name suggests, though Gloria does not find it depressing.

key_locations.name: Lincoln Theatre, Columbus
key_locations.details: The venue for the Netflix special taping. Seats 580. Historic theater. Doug has never been inside it before the rehearsal.

key_locations.name: The Thai place
key_locations.details: Where Doug and Kelsey eat when she visits. They've been going since she was fourteen. In Columbus, specific location not critical.

key_locations.name: The bar in the Short North
key_locations.details: Where Doug and Simone have dinner in chapter 15. The Short North is Columbus's arts/entertainment district.

distances: Columbus to Dayton (Gloria): approximately 70 miles, about 1 hour drive
Columbus to Cleveland (Kelsey): approximately 140 miles, about 2 hours drive
Columbus to Indianapolis (corporate gig): approximately 175 miles, about 2.5 hours drive
Columbus to Pittsburgh: approximately 185 miles, about 3 hours drive
Columbus to Detroit: approximately 200 miles, about 3.5 hours drive
Columbus to LA: cross-country flight, approximately 5 hours
Clintonville to Mutual Fidelity office: a short commute, Doug drives

world_rules: Contemporary realistic fiction — no speculative elements, no period constraints
Internet virality follows real-world mechanics: rapid growth, meme creation, comment sections, Reddit threads, journalistic coverage, brand interest
Netflix development process follows real-world general patterns: pitch meetings, development executives, content tests, taping schedules

Insurance adjusting follows real-world process: claims filed, documentation reviewed, coverage assessed, payments processed. Specific terminology (proof of loss, actual cash value, replacement cost, depreciation, coverage gap) must be used correctly
Comedy club circuit follows real-world patterns: small venues, 100-200 capacity rooms, regional touring, heckling

Doug cannot access the toast's power voluntarily — this is the novel's central constraint. The toast worked because of specific unrepeatable conditions: alcohol, Brendan's preceding toast, years of unspoken truth. Any attempt to replicate it consciously fails.

power_structure.over: Doug's public schedule and career trajectory
power_structure.mechanism: Management contract; she controls the bookings, negotiations, and public strategy
Content deal; they control production, editing, and distribution

power_structure.over: The narrative of the marriage and divorce
power_structure.mechanism: She left; she has the moral high ground of having chosen to leave, and her therapeutic composure controls how the history is discussed
Maternal bluntness; she is the only person who tells Doug exactly what she thinks without management or diplomacy
The viral video and subsequent coverage have created a version of Doug that Doug did not author and cannot control
Professional expertise; she understands comedy craft and Doug does not, giving her authority over what works and what doesn't

objects.name: The toast video
objects.origin: Recorded by an unknown wedding guest on a phone
objects.status: 80 million views and growing; exists as memes, remixes, auto-tune versions, analysis threads

objects.name: The suit
objects.origin: His one suit, worn to the wedding
objects.status: Doug wakes up in it in chapter 1; it recurs as a symbol of the night

objects.name: The management contract
objects.origin: Eleven pages, negotiated in Doug's apartment
objects.status: Active throughout; Doug won two small points, lost every large one

objects.name: The spreadsheet
objects.origin: An Excel file Doug made during the divorce tracking Megan's failures as a co-parent
objects.status: Kelsey found it at sixteen and has carried that knowledge for twelve years; revealed in chapter 13

objects.name: The boxes in the second bedroom
objects.origin: Unpacked boxes from Doug's move after the divorce, 12 years ago
objects.status: Sit unopened in the 'office' that has no computer; referenced in chapters 17 and 18 as the saddest and funniest detail
objects.chapter_introduced: chapter_06 (first mentioned), chapter_17 (performed on stage), chapter_18 (looked at)

objects.name: Megan's shampoo
objects.origin: The shampoo that was in Doug's shower; its absence was how Doug knew Megan had left
objects.status: Exists only in memory; told to Simone in chapter 11

objects.name: The art print
objects.origin: A print from a Columbus gallery that Megan left behind when she moved out
objects.status: Hangs in Doug's apartment; the one aesthetic decision in his home that he did not make

income_sources.amount: Mid-level insurance adjuster salary, roughly $55,000-65,000 annually
Smaller fees for three club appearances, likely $2,000-5,000 each
Not specified precisely, but significant enough that Gloria does the math and approves

knowledge_state.knows: He said devastating truths about his marriage, his fatherhood, and his loneliness in the toast
She found Doug's Excel spreadsheet tracking Megan's co-parenting failures when she was sixteen
The scripted material does not work and cannot work; only unscripted honesty produces comedy from him
knowledge_state.learned_in: Accumulates through chapters 9, 14; crystallizes in chapter 15
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Doug knows this but cannot operationalize it until chapter 17

knowledge_state.knows: Doug's comic voice is his literalness and specificity, not performance
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Doug (he knows intellectually but doesn't trust it)

knowledge_state.knows: The toast publicly aired details of their marriage she had contextualized privately
knowledge_state.hidden_from: No one — but her organized response in chapter 5 may conceal how much it actually affected her

knowledge_state.knows: Brendan has his own relationship to inadequacy despite appearing to have won everything
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Megan may not know Brendan feels this; Doug does not share this insight with anyone

environmental_constants: Doug's apartment in Clintonville: the Ikea bookshelf, the art print, the kitchen counter where he eats cereal, the second bedroom with unpacked boxes, the glass shower
The Mutual Fidelity office: beige open plan, thirty desks, the hum of people processing other people's small disasters, fluorescent lighting
Doug's Civic: the car he drives everywhere, unremarkable, reliable
Columbus weather tracking: cold and gray in winter chapters, gradually warming through spring to early summer warmth by the taping
The sandwich place: unchanged across nine years of lunch, the constancy of the routine
Maple Glen dining room: institutional but not hostile, the specific quality of retirement-community food and conversation
Doug's phone: the persistent intrusion — notifications, calls, texts — that represents the outside world's claim on his private life
